paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1184 English Language — AI-authored Practice Paper 23: The Friday Soup (Paper 2 §B style)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Secondary 4 girl who volunteers every Friday evening at a community kitchen in Bedok for her Community Involvement Programme hours, and her unexpected exchange with a quiet eight-year-old boy from Yangon. Answer the questions that follow. Section B [18 marks] — AI-authored practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1184/02 (formerly 1128) Section B format and calibrated to the SEAB assessment objectives (literal / inferential comprehension, vocabulary in context, language for effect). Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. Items mix mark values [1]/[2]: 9 short-answer questions (Q1-Q9) plus a 4-blank emotion-flowchart MCQ (Q10). Answer in your own words as far as possible.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
I had started for the certificate. That was the truth of it, and I did not pretend otherwise. The Sec 4 CIP hours had to be done, and a friend from church had told me about the community kitchen at the void deck of Block 124 in Bedok, where families could come for a hot meal on Friday evenings. I signed up for eight weeks. Eight weeks would be enough.

Paragraph 2
The kitchen ran out of two folding tables and a borrowed soup pot the size of a small drum. Auntie Faridah ladled rice. Uncle Raj stirred the curry. My job, the easiest one, was the soup — fishball soup, every Friday, the same recipe. I stood behind a tray of plastic bowls and waited for the queue to form at six o'clock.

Paragraph 3
The boy came on my second Friday. He was perhaps eight years old, thin in a way that made his school uniform look borrowed. His mother held his hand. She spoke softly to Auntie Faridah in an accent I later learned was Burmese. They had come from Yangon a year ago, Auntie Faridah told me afterwards, and the boy was in Primary 2 at the school nearby. His name was Wai Yan.

Paragraph 4
He did not speak. Not that first Friday, not the second, not the third. He held out his bowl with both hands, and I filled it, and he nodded once — a small, precise nod — and walked back to the table where his mother sat. I told myself it was shyness. I told myself it was the language. I did not, at first, think very much about it at all.

Paragraph 5
For two months, this was our arrangement. Six o'clock, the queue, the bowl, the nod. I learned the weight of the ladle in my wrist. I learned which fishballs sank and which floated. I learned, without realising I was learning it, the shape of Wai Yan's small hands as he reached up for his bowl, and the careful way he carried it back to his mother without spilling a single drop.

Paragraph 6
The Friday before the school holidays was the last one I had signed up for. The queue was longer than usual. The air at the void deck was thick with the smell of curry and steamed rice and the sweet damp of a coming rain. When Wai Yan reached the front of the line, I lifted the ladle as I had done seven times before, and poured the soup carefully into his bowl.

Paragraph 7
He did not walk away. He stood there, holding the bowl, and looked up at me. Then, slowly, carefully, as though each syllable had been rehearsed in front of a mirror, he said: 'Xiè xie, jie jie.' Thank you, big sister. In Mandarin. A language that was neither his nor entirely mine. I was so unprepared that I simply stared. He nodded once — that same small, precise nod — and walked back to his table.

Paragraph 8
I walked home that night through the drizzle without an umbrella. I had thought, all along, that I was the one giving. The certificate would say so. The hours would be counted. But somewhere between the second Friday and the eighth, an eight-year-old boy from Yangon had been quietly practising two words of Mandarin for a stranger at a soup pot, and I had not noticed. What I had thought I was giving, I realised, was less than what I had been receiving, week after week, without seeing it at all.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1184 §B style practice; not from a past examination paper. CIP: Community Involvement Programme, compulsory community service hours for Singapore secondary students; void deck: the open ground floor of an HDB block, often used for community functions; jie jie: Mandarin for 'older sister'.)

questions.stem: Q1. From Paragraph 1, what was the narrator's original reason for signing up at the community kitchen? [1]
questions.answer: to clock / complete her Sec 4 CIP hours for the certificate

questions.stem: Q2. What does the word 'borrowed' in 'made his school uniform look borrowed' (Paragraph 3) suggest about how the uniform fitted Wai Yan? [1]
questions.answer: it looked too big / loose on him, as if it were not his own

questions.stem: Q3. What does the word 'arrangement' (Paragraph 5) suggest about the weekly exchange between the narrator and Wai Yan? [1]
questions.answer: a fixed, settled routine repeated each week without any words

questions.stem: Q4. Using your own words, explain why the narrator did not at first think much about Wai Yan's silence. (Paragraph 4) [2]
questions.answer: ① she assumed he was simply shy; ② she put it down to a language barrier, so it did not seem worth puzzling over

questions.stem: Q5. What does the phrase 'a small, precise nod' (Paragraph 4) suggest about Wai Yan? [1]
questions.answer: he was careful / controlled and quietly polite

questions.stem: Q6. What is the effect of the phrase 'without realising I was learning it' (Paragraph 5)? [2]
questions.answer: it shows the narrator was unconsciously absorbing small details about Wai Yan, revealing she cared about him and noticed him more than she herself knew at the time

questions.stem: Q7. Using your own words, explain why Wai Yan's words of thanks were so unexpected and moving to the narrator. (Paragraph 7) [2]
questions.answer: ① after weeks of total silence he finally spoke, having clearly rehearsed the phrase; ② he thanked her in Mandarin, a language that was not his own, showing how much effort the small gesture had cost him

questions.stem: Q8. What is the effect of the writer's comparison 'as though each syllable had been rehearsed in front of a mirror' (Paragraph 7)? [2]
questions.answer: it suggests Wai Yan had deliberately and carefully practised the words beforehand, making his quiet thanks feel far more deliberate and heartfelt than a casual remark

questions.stem: Q9. Using your own words, explain what the narrator finally realised in Paragraph 8 that she had not seen while she was volunteering. [2]
questions.answer: ① she had assumed she was the one giving, but in truth she had been receiving far more; ② Wai Yan's silent kindness and rehearsed thanks were a greater gift than the soup she served

sections.instruction: Q10. The narrator's dominant feeling about her Friday evenings at the community kitchen changes as the eight weeks unfold. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best summarises her dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: routine / curious / impatient / proud / moved / changed / bored / dismissive. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Friday Soup' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift over the eight Fridays — from a businesslike, routine approach in the early weeks at the soup pot, to a small flicker of curiosity about the silent boy, to being deeply moved when he finally speaks, ending in a quiet but real change in how she sees what she has been doing. Pick the single best word from the bank for each section.

questions.stem: Q10(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 2 as she stands behind the tray of plastic bowls waiting for the queue to form at six o'clock? [1]

Q10(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 3–4 as she begins to notice Wai Yan's silence and learns from Auntie Faridah that his family arrived from Yangon a year ago? [1]
Q10(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 7 when Wai Yan looks up at her and slowly, carefully says 'Xiè xie, jie jie'? [1]

Q10(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 8 as she walks home through the drizzle, realising that what she had thought she was giving was less than what she had been receiving? [1]
